papers.title: The Link Between Ejected Stars, Hardening and Eccentricity Growth of Super Massive Black Holes in Galactic Nuclei
papers.author_names: Long Wang Peter Berczik Rainer Spurzem M.B.N. Kouwenhoven

abstract.text: The hierarchical galaxy formation picture suggests that super massive black holes (MBHs) observed in galactic nuclei today have grown from coalescence of massive black hole binaries (MBHB) after galaxy merging. Once the components of a MBHB become gravitationally bound, strong three-body encounters between the MBHB and stars dominate its evolution in a "dry" gas free environment, and change the MBHB's energy and angular momentum (semi-major axis, eccentricity and orientation). Here we present high accuracy direct N-body simulations of spherical and axisymmetric (rotating) galactic nuclei with order a million stars and two massive black holes that are initially unbound. We analyze the properties of the ejected stars due to slingshot effects from three-body encounters with the MBHB in detail. Previous studies have investigated the eccentricity and energy changes of MBHs using approximate models or Monte-Carlo three body scatterings. We find general agreement with the average results of previous semi-analytic models for spherical galactic nuclei, but our results show a large statistical variation. Our new results show many more phase space details of how the process works, and also show the influence of stellar system rotation on the process. We detect that the angle between the orbital plane of the MBHBs and that of the stellar system (when it rotates) influences the phase-space properties of the ejected stars. We also find that massive MBHB tend to switch stars with counter-rotating orbits into co-rotating orbits during their interactions.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] High-resolution $N$-body simulations of massive black hole binaries (MBHBs) in spherical and rotating galactic nuclei reveal that stellar rotation significantly alters the phase-space distribution of ejected stars and induces strong eccentricity growth via slingshot interactions. The angle between the MBHB orbital plane and the stellar system's rotation plane controls the scattering dynamics, with massive MBHBs actively converting counter-rotating stars into co-rotating ones, thereby enhancing hardening efficiency. Results show large statistical fluctuations beyond semi-analytic models, demonstrating that rotation-induced orbital mixing and asymmetric scattering are critical for resolving the Final Parsec Problem in gas-poor galaxy mergers.

source_fields.title: The Link Between Ejected Stars, Hardening and Eccentricity Growth of Super Massive Black Holes in Galactic Nuclei

source_fields.sections_text: Methods: The paper proposes a method using the $\\varphi$GPU $N$-body simulation code to identify ejected stars from massive black hole binary (MBHB) interactions by analyzing their energy changes and angular momentum, with a core idea of selecting stars that experience significant energy gains during strong encounters, defined by a critical energy ratio threshold $\\delta_e$ derived from energy distribution gaps. Results: The simulations show that in rotating King models with large black hole masses, the ejected star samples are reliably identified with negligible residual energy imbalance (ΔEᵣ(t) ≈ 0) after binary formation, indicating complete energy conservation between MBHB binding energy loss and ejected star energy gain, while low-mass MBHBs (e.g., models 0110 and 0210) exhibit incomplete samples due to undetectable low-ΔE ejections and delayed gravitational binding. Conclusions: This study demonstrates that under realistic, non-spherical conditions such as rotation or triaxiality, massive black hole binaries in galactic nuclei can overcome the Final Parsec Problem and merge within a few gigayears through superelastic scatterings and slingshot interactions, with distinct dynamical behaviors in I-model and P-model systems depending on the orientation of the binary's orbit relative to the stellar cluster's rotation. Introduction: This paper innovatively addresses the "Final Parsec Problem" by demonstrating that mildly axisymmetric or triaxial stellar environments significantly enhance MBH binary hardening rates, enabling timely coalescence within the Hubble time and providing a robust physical mechanism for detectable gravitational wave signals from massive black hole mergers. Units and Initial Conditions: The section describes a scale-free $N$-body simulation framework with rotating King models and specific initial conditions for binary black hole systems, scaled to unity gravitational constant and total mass, with detailed parameters and physical unit conversions provided for astrophysical relevance.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents N-body simulations of stellar dynamics around massive black hole binaries, analyzing ejected stars from simulations, not observational data
